titleEn: Daughter of Sakhr, She Who Weeps

verses.ajuz: لا خير في عيش وإن سرنا ،

verses.sadr: في الخيل إذ تعدو به الضافيه
verses.ajuz: يا من يرى من قومنا فارسا

translationEn: It is the daughter of Sakhr — she is the weeping one; there is no weeper tonight but her. Sakhr, who was the king of the high lands, has perished — O Abu Hassan, what grief! Woe is me — how bitter is this woe! — when the voice of the wailing crier rang out and rose above our homes with its woeful call. I disbelieved the truth, yet doubt had already seized me — until the cry of mourning rose over our houses. He was the gracious, generous chieftain who sheltered us in years of drought and hardship. Yet some of the people are cowardly and timid — envied by none, scorned even by the Bedouin. He does not speak what is right, nor does he deviate from idle music, nor does he go forth with the raiding party. When the cooking-pot is set up at his home, others crowd around a stranger's pot to beg. But my brother — he is bold, a man of firm resolve; from the likes of him the needy seek their provision. He does not utter what is vile in the presence of a free woman; he moves through the wayward ones with an untroubled heart. My brother is no mean shepherd, a weak-willed man whose heart flutters after his flocks. His garment is white and lustrous, like a flash of lightning in the dark cloudy night. Atop a swift, vigorous horse full of spirit, he leads the foremost of the resolute warriors onward. There is no good in life — even if it delights us — for time leaves nothing that endures. Every man whose family passes by him shall one day find himself cast aside. O one who would see among our people a horseman — in the thick of horses surging beneath him with flowing manes — beneath you is a broad-chested bay mare, rolled and folded like a fine Yemeni robe. When the pursuing mares catch up to her, calling out like the scattered footsteps of a swift runner, he thrusts his lance into them and turns them aside, as one levels the remaining heap of a crumbling sand mound. She swoops down when released from the watering place, like a dark eagle piercing the night clouds. A black Rudaynian spear, dark and gleaming like fire with its sharp, ready blade. The smith tempered her at her sharpening until the deadly venom was fixed within her. Where shall we find his like, now that he is gone from us — for the cavalry when they charge and for the swift runners? He swore he would not sit idle in any land far from his kin and remote. So he set out on his way, resolved, turned back by neither adviser nor forbidder.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the weeping one, the woman who weeps
the wailing announcer of death, the crier of mourning
the coming year of drought, the year of hardship
the one who begs at another's door, the petitioner
bold and awe-inspiring, a man of firm resolve
Rudaynian — a spear attributed to Rudayna, renowned for crafting fine lances
the lethal venom, the deadly poison (of the spear's tip)

context: This elegy by Al-Khansa mourns her brother Sakhr ibn Amr, contrasting his unmatched courage and generosity with the cowardice of lesser men. The poem moves from raw grief to a vivid celebration of Sakhr as warrior and horseman, culminating in the poet's despair at finding no equal to replace him.
